content.full_text:
Fern Por-rrrcAr- PnACTrcEs C oMMrssroN
-128 J Srreet o Suire 6f0 o Sacramento. CA 9-5gl.l_2329
(916) 322--5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
June 4, 2009
Ted C. Radosevich
District Counsel
2950 Peralta Oaks Court
P.O. Box 5381
Oakland, CA 94605
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-09-099
Dear Radosevich:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This letter is based on the ficts piesented; the Fair
Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when
it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because you seek general
information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2
QUESTTONS
l. Are tickets and passes provided by the East Bay Regional Park District (,.park
district") to its directors for a non-profit fundraising dinner gifts to the agency under
Regulation 18944.1or to the official when the tickets were provided to the pa.k district
by members of the community?
2. If a ticket or pass to attend a fundraising dinner for the park district is provided
directly to a district official by a member of the community is the iicket considerid a gift
to the agency or the official?
3. Are the answers to questions I and 2 any different if any employee of the park
District is provided the tickets?
' Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
I 8 I09- I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
r lnfbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
lbrmal written advice. (Section 83 I l4; regulation I g329(c)(3). copy enclosed).


File No. I-09-099
Page No. 2
4. Under Regulation 18946.4, is an official required to report receipt of a ticket
provided by a political action committee to attend a fundraising.r"nt for a candidate for
public office?
5. Under Regulation 18946.4, is an official required to report receipt of a ticket
provided by a local employee union to attend a fundraiiing event fo, u "*didute for
public office?
CONCLUSIONS
l. Tickets to nonprofit fundraising dinners are generally treated as gifts to the
official under Regulation 18946.4. Even if the "ticket'iis provided to the official by the
official's agency, Regulation L8944.1would not apply because the ticket is not for
admission to an event orfacility "for an entertainm"rri, u*rrement, recreational, or
similar purpose."
However, under certain circumstances, events that provide no benefits other than
food and beverages (the luncheon and dinner events) and where the admission is
provided by the agency to the officials who are attending the events as part of their
agency duties, Regulation 18944.2 or Regulation 189443 may apply. (See discussion
below.)
2. The ticket is considered a gift to the official and.is reportable at its full face
value' This is because the ticket is provided by a third-party to the official and not
provided by the non-profit organization that is hosting the event. Therefore, the
exception provided in Regulation 18946.4 does not apply.
3. No, unless the employee is not a statutory filer under Section g7Z0O or
designated in a class that requires reporting of income or gifts from the particular source.
If the official is not a statutory filer or a "designated empioyee," he or she does not have
any reporting obligations.
4' Under Regulation 18946.4, one ticket provided directly by the political action
committee to the official for its fundraising event, has no value.
5. Regulation 18946.4 does not apply in this case. The official must report the
full face value of the ticket because it is not offered directly by the group organizing the
fundraiser.
FACTS
You are the district counsel for the park district, a two-county special district
providing park and open-space to the residents of Contra Costa and Alameda Counties.
You seek advice on behalf of the Board of Directors, which is composed of seven elected


File No. I-09.099
Page No. 3
officials. The Regional Parks Foundation ("Foundation") is a private 501(c)(3) non-
profit that is dedicated to the sole purpose of supporting the Park District and its mission.
The Foundation holds various fundraising events such as "Diamond in the Rough.,'
Tickets for these events are purchased by park supporters, including busines"r i1th"
community. Occasionally, a business or community group will purchase a table and
provide extra tickets to board members or staff to attend the event.
Board members also occasionally receive tickets to attend political fundraising
events for individuals running for local, state, or national office. These tickets are
provided to the official either by a political action committee or local employee union.
You wish to know whether certain tickets are reportable gifts to the park district
directors and employees of the park district.
AIIALYSIS
The Act establishes a statutory and regulatory scheme to reduce influences on
public officials3 from the receipt of gifts. First, it prot lbit. cerrain public officials from
accepting large gifts (currently defined as more than $420) from identified sources.
(Section 89503, Regulation 18940.2.) Second, it requires certain public officials to
disclose their receipt of any gift of $50 or more from identified sorrcer, so that the public
is made aware of such gifts. (Sections 87207 and 87302.) Finally, it prohibits u p,rbli.
official from using his or her position to influence the outcome of a dlcision involving
the donor of a gift valued at$420 or more. (Section 87100, g7103(e), Regulation
18940.2.)
Section 82028 defines gift as
"any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the
public without regard to official status."
Your letter requests advice with respect to the gift disclosure provisions of the
Act. Public officials who are covered by the Act's gift disclosure provisions are required
to disclose gifts from certain specified sources pursuant to the terms of their agency,s
conflict-of-interest code, formulated in accordance with the Act's provisions.
Ouestions l-2: As is evident from the above definition, tickets and passes to
charitable events are "gifts" under the Act if they provide a personal benefii for which no
consideration is provided. However, several regulations under the Act may affect how
I Section defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or
local government agency. Employees of the park district are public officials.


File No. I-09-099
Page No.4
such tickets or passes are valued. For example, Regulation 18946.4 specifically
addresses valuation for tickets or passes to charitable events. It provides:
"18946.4: This regulation applies to a single ticket or other
admission privilege to a specific fundraising event provided to a pubtic
official by a nonprofit or political organization holding its own fJndraiser and
used solely by the public official.
"(a) Nonprofit Fundraiser. Except as provided in subdivision (b), the
value of a gift of a ticket, pass, or other admission privilege to a fundraising
event for a nonprofit, tax-exempt organization that is not a committee 
"oue."d
by subdivision (c) is determined as follows:
"(l) Where the ticket clearly states that a portion of the ticket price is
a donation to the organization, the value of the gift is the face value of the
ticket reduced by the amount of the donation.
"(2) lf there is no ticket indicating a face value or the ticket or other
admission privilege has no stated price or no stated donation portion, the
value ofthe gift is the pro rata share ofthe cost ofany food, and beverages,
plus any other specific item presented to the attendee at the event.
"(b) 501(c)(3) Organization Fundraiser. Where the event is a
fundraising event for an organization exempt from taxation under section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue code, the organization may provide one
ticket per event to an official and that ticket shall be deemed io have no value
so long as the cumulative value of the nondeductible portion of the ticket(s),
as provided in subdivision (a), received by the official from the same
organization during a calendar year, does not exceed the gift limits imposed
under Section 89503 and Regulation 18940.2. [Emphasis added.]
t...)"
Under this regulation, an official may receive one ticket to each fundraising event
a 501(c)(3) organization holds during a calendar year up to a cumulative value of $420,
and the ticket is not o Itft tf, and only if, the organization provides the ticket to the
fficial.
Additionally, Regulation 18944.1 (copy enclosed) provides certain exceptions to
the Act's gift rules when an official receives a ticket or pass to attend an event to perform
an official or ceremonial function related to his or her oifi.", such as accepting an award
or plaque, the admission privilege is not a gift to the official. (Regulation-l8e?+.t1a;.;
Regulation 18944.1(b) also provides a method by which certain types of tickets or
passes provided to an official by his or her agency will not be considered gifts because


File No. I-09-099
Page No. 5
the official will meet the burden, under the Act's definition of gift, that equal or greater value
has been provided in exchange therefor, as discussed below. However, this regulation
applies only to tickets or passes provided to the official by his or her ugerr"yio an event
or facility "for an entertainment, amusement, recreational, or similar pu.por".;' Therefore,
the regulation would only apply to tickets or passes provided by your agency to officials in
the agency to attend "charitable balls, golf tournaments, and music festivals," but not to
"luncheons and dinners." (Scott Advice [rtter, I-09-104.)
In your request for advice, you state that the events to which you refer are
charitable fundraising events where sponsors or individuals purchase either a table or a
seat for a luncheon or dinner. The event you refer to in your iequest is for a non-profit
"fundraising dinner" and not an event "for entertainment, recreational, or similar pr.por".
Accordingly, the provisions of Regulation 18944.1 would not apply even if the
"ticket" is provided to the official by the official's agency. Regulation l}g44.l would
not apply because, as explained above, the "ticket" is not for an admission to an event or
facility "for an entertainment, amusement, recreational, or similar purpose." Regulation
18946.4 would normally apply in these cases.
Fundraisers: FPPC Regulation 18946.4 as Amended," which prouider more detailed
information.
However, with respect to events that provide no benefits other than food and
beverages (the luncheon and dinner events) and where the admission is provided by the
agency to the officials who are attending the events as part of their ug"n"y duties,
Regulation 18944.2 or Regulation 18944.3 may apply.
Regulation 18944.2 (copy enclosed) applies to payments, other than tickets and
passes for entertainment, recreational and similar events covered under Regulation
18944.1' made to an agency from an outside source that are used for the prilpose of
agency business. If the agency follows the requirements of Regulati on 18944.2, there is
no gift to the official. Assuming the official is attending a luncheon or dinner on official
agency business, Regulation 18944.2 could apply to these charity events.
. 
Additionally, Regulation 18944.3, which is applicable when an agency expends
its own resources,' could apply if the agency provides to its officials tickets iti purchases
from the charitable organization with its own funds. This regulation would apply if the
payment for the meal at the event is a lawful expenditure of public moneys, unOtt "
official's meal would not be a gift under the Act.
* Other that tickets to events under Regulation l g9zl4. l.


File No. I-09-099
Page No. 6
Tickets or Passes Provided Directly to the Official by Outside Sources
In your request for advice, you indicate that admissions to the charitable
fundraisers are sometimes provided directly to park district officials by members of the
community who have purchased them directly from the charitable organization.
In these cases, the value of the ticket is the full face value of the purchase,
including the amount of the donation.
If the tickets are provided directly from the nonprofit organization to the officials,
Regulation 18946.4 would apply as follows: If the nonprofit organization is exempt from
taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Intemal Revenue Code, the tickets or admiision
has no value so long as the portion of the ticket value that is not tax-deductible (either by
itself or when cumulated with the value of other gifts from the organization in the same
calendar year) does not exceed the $420 gift limit applicable to th; official. If that
portion exceeds the limit, the official cannot accept the ticket.
If the nonprofit organization is not a 501(c)(3) organization, the value of the ticket
is the non-deductible portion of the ticket value or if there is no ticket or the tax-
deductible portion is not stated, the value is the pro-rata share of the cost of any food and
beverages plus any specific item provided to the attendee at the event. This value also
cannot exceed the $420 gift limit applicable to the official.5
Q..uestiq4,3: The provisions of Regulation 18944.1 and 18946.4 apply to all those who have
filing obligations under the Act. This includes both statutory fileri-under Section g72OO and,
those agency "designated employees" who file pursuant to an agency's adopted conflict-of-
interest code.
If the official is not a statutory filer or a "designated employee" required to report
income or gifts from the source, he or she does not have any reporting obligations.
Ouestions 4 and 5: Regulation 18946.4(c) provides:
"Political Fundraiser. For a gift of a ticket, pass, or other admission privilege
to a fundraising event for a committee defined in section g2ol3(a), or a
comparable committee regulated under federal law holding an event in
California, the committee or candidate may provide one ti;ket per event to an
official and that ticket shall be deemed to have no value."
Therefore, the official may accept one ticketp rovided directly by the political
action committee to the official for its fundraising event. However, if the ticket to the
sRegulation 18944' I (bX l) permits an official to treat a ricker or pass provided to the official by his
or her agency as income, and not a gift, if done in a manner consistent wiih applicable tax laws- The value
of the ticket or pass is its face value if it is treated as income.


File No. I-09-099
Page No. 7
political fundraising event is provided by a third-party to the official, the official must
report the full face value of the ticket and is subject to the $420 gift limit if applicable for
that official to the source of the gift.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me
at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Commission Counsel
ERjgl
Enclosures
3

Box 5381 Oakland, CA 94605 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No.I-09-099 Dear Radosevich: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This letter is based on the ficts piesented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because you seek general information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 QUESTTONS l. Are tickets and passes provided by the East Bay Regional Park District (,.park district") to its directors for a non-profit fundraising dinner gifts to the agency under Regulation 18944.1or to the official when the tickets were provided to the pa.k district by members of the community? 2. If a ticket or pass to attend a fundraising dinner for the park district is provided directly to a district official by a member of the community is the iicket considerid a gift to the agency or the official? 3. Are the answers to questions I and 2 any different if any employee of the park District is provided the tickets? ' Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections I 8 I09- I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations. r lnfbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or lbrmal written advice. (Section 83 I l4; regulation I g329(c)(3). copy enclosed). File No. I-09-099 Page No. 2 4. Under Regulation 18946.4, is an official required to report receipt of a ticket provided by a political action committee to attend a fundraising.r"nt for a candidate for public office? 5. Under Regulation 18946.4, is an official required to report receipt of a ticket provided by a local employee union to attend a fundraiiing event fo, u "*didute for public office? CONCLUSIONS l. Tickets to nonprofit fundraising dinners are generally treated as gifts to the official under Regulation 18946.4. Even if the "ticket'iis provided to the official by the official's agency, Regulation L8944.1would not apply because the ticket is not for admission to an event orfacility "for an entertainm"rri, u*rrement, recreational, or similar purpose." However, under certain circumstances, events that provide no benefits other than food and beverages (the luncheon and dinner events) and where the admission is provided by the agency to the officials who are attending the events as part of their agency duties, Regulation 18944.2 or Regulation 189443 may apply. (See discussion below.) 2. The ticket is considered a gift to the official and.is reportable at its full face value' This is because the ticket is provided by a third-party to the official and not provided by the non-profit organization that is hosting the event.
